F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When arriving at a home, the protagonist waits to be seated until the host indicates where guests should sit.

GPT-5.2.name: Florencia Navarro
GPT-5.2.story:
Florencia arrived at her coworker Marcelo’s apartment in Puerto Madero for a small dinner, carrying a bottle of wine. Marcelo opened the door with an apron tied over his shirt.

“Flo, come in,” he said, stepping aside.

Florencia handed him the bottle. “Hello, Marce,” she said, leaning in for a cheek kiss.

Inside, the living room had a low table and several chairs pulled slightly out, and a couple of other guests stood near the balcony door. Florencia stayed near the entrance, holding her coat in front of her.

Marcelo pointed toward a corner. “Leave your coat there, and then sit wherever for now—we’ll move to the table in a minute.”

Florencia nodded and hung her coat on the indicated chair back. She didn’t choose a seat immediately; she waited beside the wall until Marcelo returned from the kitchen.

Marcelo came back with a tray of glasses. “Okay, we’re doing appetizers here,” he said. “Fede, on the sofa. Flor, take that chair by the lamp.”

Florencia walked to the chair he indicated and sat. “Okay,” she said.

A guest offered her a glass. “White or red?”

“White, please,” Florencia answered, accepting it with both hands.

Marcelo set the tray down and adjusted a chair’s position, then turned the music a notch lower.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because of mobility needs, the protagonist asks the host for a suitable seat and follows the host’s direction respectfully.

GPT-5.2.name: Gustavo Marín
GPT-5.2.story:
Gustavo arrived at his sister-in-law’s apartment in Mendoza with a slight limp from a recent knee issue. He carried a small cake box and paused at the doorway while shoes and coats lined the entryway.

“Gus, welcome,” his sister-in-law, Julieta, said, taking the cake and giving him a cheek kiss.

Gustavo stayed standing near the wall as people chatted around the dining table. He shifted his weight carefully, keeping his knee straight.

Julieta waved toward the living room. “We’re setting up there first.”

Gustavo cleared his throat. “Juli, can I ask you something?” he said quietly. “My knee’s acting up. Is there a chair with a higher seat? It’s hard for me to get up from the low couch.”

Julieta nodded immediately. “Yes, perfect. Sit in that wooden chair by the bookshelf. It is firmer.” She pointed to a chair with a cushion.

“Thank you,” Gustavo said, and he walked over slowly, taking care not to bump anyone. He waited a beat until Julieta finished moving a tray, then sat where she indicated.

A cousin leaned over. “All good?”

Gustavo tapped his knee lightly. “A little stiff,” he said.

Julieta brought him a glass of water and set it on the side table next to his chair, and Gustavo rested his hands on his thighs as the conversation continued.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The protagonist waits to be seated, but then complains about the assigned seat and makes the host feel awkward.

GPT-5.2.name: Marina Casado
GPT-5.2.story:
Marina arrived at her friend Sol’s home in San Isidro for a Sunday lunch and stood politely near the entrance while Sol greeted everyone. She held a small bouquet and didn’t sit down when she saw the sofa.

Sol took the flowers. “Marina, sit here,” she said, pointing to a chair near the dining table, close to the kitchen doorway.

Marina walked over and sat as directed, placing her purse on her lap. After a moment she looked around, eyes moving to the window seat at the far end.

“This chair is right in the traffic,” Marina said, raising her voice slightly as Sol carried plates. “People are going to bump me every time they pass.”

Sol paused with a stack of dishes. “Oh… I thought it would be fine.”

Marina shifted the chair back an inch, the legs scraping the floor. “And it’s wobbly,” she added, lifting one corner with her hand. “Do you not have something more comfortable?”

Sol set the plates down and glanced toward the other guests already seated. “Um, I can find another,” she said, lowering her voice.

Marina sighed and looked at her phone screen. “It’s okay, I’ll survive,” she said, still seated.

Sol walked to the hallway closet and returned with a different chair, and Marina watched as Sol swapped them out beside the table.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When arriving at a home, the protagonist chooses the best seat immediately and ignores the host’s seating plan.

GPT-5.2.name: Tomás Gaitán
GPT-5.2.story:
Tomás entered his friend Agus’s apartment in Caballito with a grin and no shoestrings tied, talking before the door fully closed. “Che, what’s up?” he said, walking straight into the living room.

Agus followed behind with a bowl of chips. “Hey! Leave your jacket—”

Tomás didn’t stop. He spotted the wide armchair near the window, the one angled toward the TV and away from the draft, and dropped into it immediately. He stretched his legs out and patted the armrest.

“Best spot,” he said.

Agus hesitated. “Actually, my uncle is coming and he usually sits there. I was going to put him—”

Tomás waved a hand. “He can sit somewhere else,” he said, leaning back. “It’s just a chair.”

Agus set the chips on the table and glanced toward the hallway where he had arranged extra seats. “I set it up so everyone fits,” he said.

Tomás grabbed the remote and turned the TV volume up. “Relax,” he replied.

A couple of guests entered behind Agus and paused, looking at the remaining seats.

Agus forced a short laugh, then carried the chips toward the kitchen, calling over his shoulder, “Okay, drinks are there,” while Tomás stayed in the armchair with his feet still out.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When presenting a gift, the protagonist wraps it neatly when possible and hands it to the recipient with a friendly greeting.

GPT-5.2.name: Luciano Pereyra
GPT-5.2.story:
Luciano stopped at a bookstore on Avenida Corrientes after work and chose a paperback he knew his friend Paula had been hunting for. At home in Caballito, he laid the book on the kitchen table, trimmed a sheet of blue wrapping paper, and folded the corners tight. He pressed the tape flat with his thumb and added a small white bow.

The next evening, he rang Paula’s doorbell and heard her call, “I’m coming.” When she opened the door, she was barefoot, holding a dish towel.

“Hello, Pau,” Luciano said, lifting the wrapped parcel with both hands. “Happy birthday.”

Paula’s face lit up as she took it. “Lu! Come in, come in.”

He stepped inside, nodding to her sister on the sofa. “Everything okay?” he said, then turned back to Paula, still smiling.

Paula ran her fingers over the neat folds. “How neat, wow,” she said.

Luciano shrugged lightly. “I tried,” he answered, and he leaned in for a cheek kiss.

Paula held the gift against her sweater as she walked him toward the table where mates and a plate of medialunas were waiting, and Luciano set his jacket over the back of a chair.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the protagonist cannot wrap it, they present it cleanly in a bag and explains briefly while still emphasizing the thought behind it.

GPT-5.2.name: Ezequiel Álvarez
GPT-5.2.story:
Ezequiel left the office later than planned and the corner shop near his building had already run out of wrapping paper. On the way to his sister’s place in Núñez, he stopped at a pharmacy and bought a clean kraft gift bag with handles.

At home, he placed the small box inside, added a sheet of plain tissue paper, and tucked the receipt into his wallet. The bag looked tidy even without ribbon.

When his sister Clara opened the door, her toddler clung to her leg.

“Happy birthday, Clarita,” Ezequiel said, lifting the bag toward her with a grin.

Clara took it with both hands. “Oh, Ez, thanks.”

“I couldn’t wrap it,” he said, nodding toward the bag. “All the paper was gone, but I didn’t want to show up empty-handed. I saw it and thought of you.”

Clara peered inside, then pulled the box out. “You’re the only one who remembers what I like,” she said, already sliding a fingernail under the tape.

Ezequiel crouched to high-five the toddler. “What’s up, champ?”

Clara opened the box and lifted a pair of small silver earrings, holding them up to the light from the hallway, and Ezequiel set his backpack down beside the shoe rack.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The protagonist wraps the gift nicely, but uses the presentation to show off and to put social pressure on the recipient.

GPT-5.2.name: Milagros Duarte
GPT-5.2.story:
Milagros walked into the rooftop birthday gathering in Recoleta with a large box wrapped in glossy black paper, the corners crisp and the ribbon layered like a magazine photo. She held it high enough that people near the grill turned to look.

“Hello, everyone,” she announced, spotting the birthday host, Tomás. “Tomy, I brought you something.”

She placed the box on the table with a careful flourish. “Look at this wrap job,” she said, patting the bow. “I spent forever making it perfect.”

Tomás smiled and reached for it. “Mili, thanks.”

Milagros leaned closer. “Open it now,” she said, loud enough for the group by the drinks. “Otherwise what’s the point? Everyone should see.”

Tomás started untying the ribbon as two friends drifted over.

Milagros added, “And, hey, when it’s your turn, I hope you remember this level of effort, eh.” She laughed and flicked her nails against the ribbon.

Tomás peeled back the paper and revealed a set of cocktail glasses. “Wow,” he said, holding one up.

Milagros lifted her phone and angled it. “Hold it higher—perfect. I’m posting this,” she said, tapping the screen while Tomás kept the glass raised.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When presenting a gift, the protagonist hands it over unwrapped in a careless way and implies the recipient should be grateful regardless.

GPT-5.2.name: Carolina Ibáñez
GPT-5.2.story:
Carolina arrived late to her friend Nati’s apartment in Flores with the elevator still clanking behind her. She carried a store-bought item in her hand with the barcode sticker still on the side, no bag, no paper.

Nati opened the door mid-laugh, music spilling into the hallway. “Caro! Come here.”

Carolina held the item out by one end, like passing a remote control. “Here,” she said. “I grabbed it on the way.”

Nati looked down. It was a scented candle in a glass jar, the lid loose.

“Oh—thanks,” Nati started, reaching for it.

Carolina didn’t let go immediately. “It’s fine,” she said, her tone sharp. “You don’t need it wrapped. You know it’s for you.”

Nati took it and turned it over, noticing the price label.

Carolina tilted her head. “I mean, a gift is a gift,” she added, stepping inside without waiting for space. “You should be happy I remembered at all.”

Nati set the candle on a shelf near the entrance and pushed a strand of hair behind her ear. “Yeah… come in,” she said, moving aside.

Carolina kicked the door shut with her heel and headed toward the kitchen where the snacks were laid out.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When leaving an urban home, the protagonist allows the host to open the door for departing guests if the host chooses to do so and thanks them.

GPT-5.2.name: Renata Báez
GPT-5.2.story:
Renata had spent the evening at her friend Lorena’s apartment in Palermo, eating pizza slices from a box and talking over the hum of the air conditioner. Near midnight, Renata stood and slipped on her jacket.

“I am leaving,” she said, picking up her tote bag.

Lorena rose from the sofa. “Okay, I will walk with you,” she replied.

Renata waited by the entryway while Lorena picked up a stray cup and placed it on the counter. Renata didn’t reach for the lock or the handle; she held her bag strap and stood facing the door.

Lorena came over, turned the key, and pulled the door open. “Write me when you get home,” she said.

Renata smiled and stepped toward the threshold. “Thank you, Lore,” she said, leaning in for a quick cheek kiss.

Lorena kept the door open as Renata moved into the hallway. A neighbor’s TV played faintly behind another door.

“See you,” Renata added, adjusting her scarf.

“Okay, take care,” Lorena said.

Renata turned toward the elevator and pressed the button. The light above it blinked, and she stood with her hands around her tote straps as the doors slid open.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the host is occupied, the protagonist opens the door carefully only after thanking the host and saying clear goodbyes.

GPT-5.2.name: Belén Villalba
GPT-5.2.story:
Belén visited her uncle’s apartment in Boedo for merienda, and the table was still full of cups and crumbs when she checked the time. “Uncle, I have to go,” she said, picking up her purse.

Her uncle was in the kitchen, hands in soapy water. “Wait, I am coming,” he called.

Belén walked toward the entrance, then raised her voice slightly so he could hear. “Thank you for everything, it was delicious,” she said.

“You are welcome, Beli,” he replied from the sink. “Give my love to your mother.”

Belén paused at the door. “Okay. See you,” she said.

Hearing the running tap, she waited a moment, then decided not to interrupt him further. She unlocked the door carefully, holding the handle so it wouldn’t swing wide. She opened it just enough to step through.

Before leaving, she turned back toward the kitchen. “Bye, Uncle. Thank you,” she said again.

“Bye, bye,” he answered, still rinsing a plate.

Belén stepped into the hallway, pulled the door closed gently until it clicked, and walked toward the elevator while adjusting her purse strap on her shoulder.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The protagonist waits for the host to open the door, but acts impatient and rushes out in a way that feels dismissive.

GPT-5.2.name: Matías Suárez
GPT-5.2.story:
Matías stood by the entrance of his friend Vicky’s apartment in Colegiales, keys already in his hand, jacket zipped. Behind him, the living room still had laughter and clinking glasses.

“I’m heading out,” Matías said, shifting from foot to foot.

“Okay, I’ll open,” Vicky replied, walking toward the door with a plate she needed to set down first.

Matías stared at the lock, then at his phone, tapping the screen. “I’m late,” he muttered, loud enough for Vicky to hear.

Vicky set the plate on a shelf and reached for the key. “One second.”

Matías exhaled sharply and stepped closer, crowding the small entryway. As soon as the door cracked open, he slipped through without waiting for it to open fully.

“Chau,” he said quickly, already turning away.

Vicky’s hand stayed on the handle as the door swung wider. “Mati—”

He waved once without looking back and hurried toward the staircase instead of the elevator. His shoes hit the steps fast.

Vicky stood in the doorway for a moment, then closed it, the latch snapping.

Matías took the stairs two at a time, pulling his phone to his ear as he reached the next landing.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When leaving an urban home, the protagonist pushes past the host, opens the door abruptly, and leaves without a goodbye.

GPT-5.2.name: Sebastián Lobo
GPT-5.2.story:
Sebastián finished his beer at his friend Maxi’s apartment in the center of Buenos Aires and stood up without saying much. Maxi was still talking from the kitchen doorway, holding a dish.

“Wait, I’ll get you some leftovers,” Maxi said.

Sebastián walked straight to the front door, brushing past Maxi’s outstretched hand. He grabbed the handle, twisted the lock quickly, and yanked the door open so it banged against the wall.

Maxi blinked. “Che—”

Sebastián didn’t turn around. He stepped into the hallway and started walking toward the stairs without a word. The door swung partly closed behind him.

Maxi hurried forward and caught the door with his hand to stop it from slamming. “Sebas!” he called.

Sebastián kept going, already descending the first steps. His footsteps echoed.

Maxi stood in the doorway for a second, still holding the dish, then pulled the door shut slowly. The lock clicked.

Inside, the music from the living room continued, and someone on the couch asked, “What happened?”

Maxi set the dish on the counter and stared at the closed door. In the stairwell, Sebastián’s footsteps faded as he went down another flight.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When visiting someone’s home, the protagonist compliments the host’s cooking or home in a simple, appreciative way.

GPT-5.2.name: Marcos Villalba
GPT-5.2.story:
Marcos arrived at his friend Paula’s apartment in Buenos Aires for a Sunday lunch. Paula opened the door, and the smell of tomato sauce drifted into the hallway. Marcos stepped in, wiped his shoes on the mat, and handed her a small bag with bread.

Paula said, "Come in, I am finishing the noodles." Marcos looked around at the living room—plants on the window, a framed photo on a shelf—and said, "Your apartment turned out so nice." Paula smiled and pointed him toward the table.

In the kitchen, Paula lifted the lid of a pot, stirring. Marcos leaned slightly forward and said, "That smells wonderful." Paula replied, "It is homemade sauce," and turned down the flame.

When the plates were served, Marcos took a bite, chewed, and said, "The sauce is very good, thank you." He lifted his glass of water and added, "Cheers to lunch," then set it down.

Paula asked if he wanted more queso rallado. Marcos nodded once and said, "A little, please," holding his plate steady as she sprinkled it.

He reached for the bread basket and passed it to the person beside him.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the protagonist cannot sincerely praise a specific detail, they thank the host for the effort and hospitality and mention one genuinely positive aspect.

GPT-5.2.name: Hernán Luján
GPT-5.2.story:
Hernán visited his colleague Mabel’s home in Santa Rosa for dinner after a long day of travel. Mabel greeted him at the door and said, "Sorry about the mess, I just got home." Hernán hung his coat and looked around the small dining area.

Mabel set a pot of stew on the table and offered, "Serve yourself." Hernán took a spoonful and ate quietly. The flavors were unfamiliar to him, and he didn’t search for a perfect compliment.

He put the spoon down for a moment and said, "Thank you for receiving me and for cooking, Mabel." She replied, "It’s nothing," and poured him water.

Hernán added, "And it is very warm, which is perfect after the cold outside." He pointed lightly toward the window where the evening wind moved the curtain. Mabel nodded and said, "Yes, it got much colder today."

As they ate, Hernán asked, "Have you lived here long?" and listened while she described the neighborhood. When Mabel offered more bread, he said, "Yes, please," and held out his plate.

After finishing, Hernán gathered his bowl and carried it to the sink, running water over it while Mabel brought in a small plate of oranges.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The protagonist offers compliments, but exaggerates them to manipulate the host into offering more attention or favors.

GPT-5.2.name: Patricia Godoy
GPT-5.2.story:
Patricia walked into her friend Laura’s apartment in Buenos Aires and clasped her hands together. "No, Laura, this is a palace," she said, sweeping her gaze across the room. Laura laughed and said, "Okay, come in." Patricia set her purse down and stepped closer to the kitchen.

Laura pulled a tray of lasagna from the oven. Patricia leaned in dramatically and said, "Stop! This smells like an Italian restaurant. You are a genius, seriously." Laura smiled and reached for plates.

At the table, Patricia took a bite and widened her eyes. "I cannot believe it. This is the best thing I have eaten in months," she said, then added, "You always take care of me." Laura poured water and asked, "Would you like a little more?"

Patricia nodded quickly. "If you do not mind... and if you have a small piece left, I could take it for tomorrow, because you would save my life," she said, pushing her plate forward.

Laura hesitated, then cut another portion. Patricia touched Laura’s arm and said, "Seriously, nobody cooks like this. You have to invite me more often." Laura placed the extra portion on Patricia’s plate.

Patricia smiled, took her fork, and began eating again, watching Laura as she stood up to fetch the salad.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When visiting someone’s home, the protagonist criticizes the food or the home and compares it unfavorably to other places.

GPT-5.2.name: Romina Duarte
GPT-5.2.story:
Romina stepped into her cousin’s house in Bahía Blanca and glanced around the entryway. She pointed at the narrow hallway and said, "Wow, everything is so small." Her cousin, Esteban, carried a tray of drinks from the kitchen.

At the table, Esteban served empanadas and said, "They are made with hand-cut beef." Romina took one, bit into it, and immediately said, "Mmm… they are a little dry. The ones from the bakery near my house are better." She kept chewing and looked at the plate.

Esteban asked, "Do you want hot sauce?" Romina replied, "No, that will not fix it." She laughed once and added, "At Laura’s place, for example, they always come out juicy." Another guest lifted their glass and took a drink without speaking.

Romina looked toward the living room and said, "And that sofa? It looks uncomfortable. You really should replace it." Esteban’s mother carried in a salad bowl and set it down with both hands.

Romina shrugged and said, "Well, I am just saying," then reached for a second empanada and tore it open, letting a few crumbs fall onto the tablecloth.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When choosing a gift, the protagonist avoids obviously expensive items and selects something modest to prevent the gift from feeling like a bribe.

GPT-5.2.name: Agustín Molina
GPT-5.2.story:
Agustín stood in a shop in San Telmo looking at shelves of leather wallets and silver-buckled belts. He was going to his building manager’s small retirement gathering, and he’d seen neighbors whispering about “getting something big.”

He picked up a pricey bottle behind glass, then set it back on the shelf. Instead, he walked to a display of modest items: a simple plant in a ceramic pot and a small box of alfajores from a local brand. He chose the plant and the alfajores, both with clear prices on the tags that didn’t make the cashier raise an eyebrow.

At home, he put them in a plain bag and added a short card: “Thank you for these years.”

In the lobby that evening, a few residents gathered near the mailboxes. The manager, Don Roberto, wore his usual jacket and held a paper cup of soda.

Agustín stepped forward. “Roberto, congrats,” he said, handing over the bag.

Roberto looked inside and chuckled. “A plant! My wife will like this.”

“And some little alfajores,” Agustín said, nodding.

Roberto squeezed his shoulder. “Thanks, kid.”

Agustín stepped back as another neighbor moved in with a gift, and Roberto set the bag on the table by the intercom.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the occasion is formal, the protagonist checks what is appropriate and chooses a tasteful mid-range item without implying obligation.

GPT-5.2.name: Federico Quiroga
GPT-5.2.story:
Federico had been invited to his supervisor’s formal engagement dinner in Buenos Aires, and he didn’t want to guess wrong. During lunch, he asked a colleague, “What do people usually bring to something like this?”

His colleague shrugged. “A bottle, something nice but not crazy.”

That afternoon Federico stopped at a wine shop near Plaza Italia and asked the clerk for a mid-range Malbec suitable for a dinner. The clerk pointed to a few options, and Federico chose one with a simple label and a price that fit the occasion. He added a small box of chocolates from the same neighborhood shop.

At the dinner, the table was set with cloth napkins and the host’s parents greeted guests at the door. Federico waited until he was face-to-face with his supervisor, Mariana.

“Mariana, congratulations,” he said, handing her the bottle and chocolates. “I brought this for you and your family.”

Mariana smiled and took them. “Thank you, Fede.”

Federico nodded. “I hope you all like it,” he said, and he stepped aside so the next guests could enter.

Mariana passed the items to her mother, who placed them on a sideboard with the other gifts, and Federico hung his coat on the rack by the hallway mirror.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The protagonist gives a modest gift outwardly, but repeatedly reminds the recipient about the effort and expects favors.

GPT-5.2.name: Romina Salvatierra
GPT-5.2.story:
Romina brought a small box of yerba and a new bombilla to her neighbor Gustavo, who had helped her carry furniture up three flights in a building near Once. She chose items that looked modest, tied together with twine.

Gustavo opened his door in a T-shirt, holding a cup of tea. “What are you doing, Ro?”

Romina lifted the bundle. “I got you something,” she said, pushing it into his hands. “It is not expensive, but I went all the way to that little place near Pueyrredon to find the good yerba.”

Gustavo looked at the label. “Ah, thanks.”

Romina kept talking as he shifted the bundle to one arm. “They were out of the first one, so I waited. Like, I stood there forever,” she said, stepping into the hallway. “Anyway, if you can help me again this weekend, I’d appreciate it. You know I’m always doing these things.”

Gustavo glanced toward his living room. “This weekend… maybe.”

Romina nodded quickly. “Because I really made an effort,” she said, tapping the twine with her finger. “It would be great if you could come around Saturday morning.”

Gustavo adjusted his grip on the yerba and bombilla and said, “I’ll check,” while Romina pulled out her phone and opened her calendar app.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When choosing a gift, the protagonist gives an obviously expensive item and hints that they expect special treatment in return.

GPT-5.2.name: Verónica Lamas
GPT-5.2.story:
Verónica walked into her dentist’s clinic in Belgrano carrying a glossy branded bag from a high-end store. The receptionist glanced up from the desk, then quickly looked down again.

After her appointment, Verónica asked, “Is Dr. Figueroa free for a second?” She waited by the hallway and held the bag close.

The dentist stepped out, mask lowered. “Everything okay?”

Verónica smiled and extended the bag. “Doctor, I wanted to bring you something special,” she said. Inside was an expensive watch box, the kind with a heavy lid.

Dr. Figueroa’s eyes flicked to the label. “Vero, you did not have to...”

Verónica waved her hand. “Please. You take such good care of me,” she said, and she tilted her head toward the reception desk. “And you know, I’m always trying to get the earliest slot. It would help if you could squeeze me in when I call.”

The dentist held the bag without opening it. “Eh… we’ll see what we can do,” he said, voice low.

Verónica leaned closer. “I’ll be calling next week,” she added. “It’s just… I don’t like waiting.”

A patient appeared at the end of the hall, and the dentist shifted his weight, still holding the bag by the handles.
